meaning: Attacking an opponent's character or personal traits instead of engaging with their actual argument. Analogy: Throwing mud at a runner because you cannot run faster than them.
example: We should reject the doctor's advice on nutrition because she was once caught speeding in a school zone.

meaning: Misrepresenting or exaggerating an opponent's argument to make it easier to attack or defeat. Analogy: Building a weak dummy out of straw just so you can easily knock it down and claim you won a fight.
example: My opponent wants to reduce the police budget, which clearly means she wants to let criminals run wild and destroy our city.

meaning: Arguing that a relatively small first step will inevitably lead to a chain of negative events. Analogy: Refusing to let a child choose their own dessert because you believe it will lead to them refusing all healthy food and ending up malnourished.
example: If we allow employees to work from home on Fridays, they will soon demand to work from home all week, and eventually our entire company will go out of business.

meaning: Introducing an irrelevant topic to divert attention from the original argument. Analogy: Dragging a smelly fish across a trail to throw tracking dogs off the scent of the actual prey.
example: The senator was asked about his tax evasion charges, but he responded by talking about the importance of family values.

meaning: Dismissing an opponent's argument by pointing out that their own behavior is inconsistent with their conclusion. Analogy: Rejecting your parent's warning about the dangers of smoking because they used to smoke when they were younger.
example: How can you tell me to stop wasting money when you just bought an expensive new television last week?

meaning: Insisting a claim is true solely because an authority figure said so, especially when that authority is not an expert in the relevant field. Analogy: Believing a foot doctor's advice on how to repair a car engine.
example: The famous actor says this vitamin supplement cures insomnia, so it must be effective.

meaning: Arguing that a claim is true or an action is correct simply because many people believe or do it. Analogy: Jumping onto a crowded train carriage just because everyone else is running toward it, without checking where it goes.
example: Everyone is investing in this new cryptocurrency, so it must be a safe and profitable investment.

meaning: Using personal experiences or isolated examples to refute statistical data or scientific evidence. Analogy: Arguing that seatbelts are dangerous because your uncle survived a crash because he wasn't wearing one.
example: The news says global temperatures are rising, but it was freezing in my backyard today, so global warming is a hoax.

meaning: Arguing that because something is natural it is good or healthy, and because something is unnatural it is bad or harmful. Analogy: Eating poisonous mushrooms because they grew wild in the forest, while refusing life-saving synthetic medicine.
example: This herbal supplement is made from natural mountain plants, so it cannot have any harmful side effects.

meaning: Judging the validity or quality of an idea based solely on its origin or history rather than its current merits. Analogy: Refusing to buy a reliable car because the car company originally made military vehicles a century ago.
example: The ideas behind that educational program were first proposed in a tabloid newspaper, so the program cannot be useful.

meaning: Arguing that a belief is true or false based on whether its outcomes are desirable or undesirable. Analogy: Refusing to believe your house has termites because if it did, you would have to pay for expensive repairs.
example: There must be life after death, because the alternative of fading into nothingness is too depressing to accept.

meaning: Arguing that a practice or belief is correct simply because it has been followed for a long time. Analogy: Refusing to use a tractor because your family has plowed fields with horses for generations.
example: Our company has always printed reports on paper; there is no reason to switch to digital files now.

meaning: Arguing that an idea or product is superior simply because it is new or modern. Analogy: Upgrading to a new phone model that has fewer features just because it was released this week.
example: This new teaching method was designed last month, so it is bound to produce better results than older methods.

meaning: Arguing that because something is a certain way in nature, it ought to be that way in human society. Analogy: Arguing that because wild animals steal food from each other, humans should be allowed to steal without punishment.
example: In nature, only the strongest survive, so we should not have social safety nets for the poor or sick.

meaning: Arguing that because something ought to be a certain way, it is that way in reality. Analogy: Believing that because crime is bad, no crimes are actually committed in your neighborhood.
example: Since warfare is destructive and immoral, humans must not possess any biological instincts for aggression.

meaning: An argument that may be valid in itself but proves a conclusion other than the one it was meant to prove. Analogy: Giving a detailed speech about how delicious soup is to prove that soup is the most affordable food in the world.
example: The developer argued that the new building would be extremely safe, but the debate was about whether the building would block sunlight.

meaning: Demanding new or more rigorous evidence after the initial criteria for proof have already been met. Analogy: Changing the location of the finish line after a runner has already crossed the original one.
example: You proved the drug is safe in laboratory trials, but now you must prove it has no side effects over a hundred years.

meaning: Applying a double standard by claiming an exception to a rule for oneself or a favored group without providing a valid justification. Analogy: A politician writing a law against speeding but expecting the police to ignore their own speeding because they are 'too busy.'
example: Everyone should pay taxes to support public services, but my business shouldn't have to because we do great work.

meaning: Selecting only the evidence that supports your position while ignoring a large body of contradictory evidence. Analogy: Searching a basket of apples and pulling out the only three ripe ones to prove the entire harvest is ready.
example: This study shows that one cup of coffee improved memory in five people, so coffee cures memory loss.

meaning: Discrediting an opponent or source before they even have a chance to present their argument. Analogy: Pouring poison into a well before people drink from it so they will reject whatever comes out.
example: Before you listen to my opponent's speech, keep in mind that he is a known liar who only cares about money.

meaning: Rejecting a claim or discrediting a person because they are associated with an unpopular group or individual. Analogy: Assuming a dog is aggressive just because it walks in the same park as a wolf.
example: You agree with some tax policies of that socialist country, which means you must support their totalitarian government.

meaning: Arguing that someone's wealth or financial success makes their conclusions more likely to be true. Analogy: Believing a billionaire's advice on how to raise children simply because they are incredibly rich.
example: He is a self-made multi-millionaire, so his ideas about quantum physics must be correct.

meaning: Arguing that someone's poverty or humble lifestyle makes their conclusions more virtuous or true. Analogy: Assuming a monk's advice on running a business is correct because he owns no material possessions.
example: The villagers live simple, impoverished lives close to the earth, so their traditional medicine is superior to modern science.

meaning: Thinking that ideas, practices, or technologies of the past are automatically inferior to those of the present. Analogy: Assuming that a modern electronic book is better written than a classical Greek manuscript just because it is newer.
example: People in the nineteenth century didn't have computers, so their philosophy on ethics has no relevance to our lives.
